original_abstract:
The Folin-Ciocalteau (F-C) assay remains the most accessible and widely utilized method for quantifying total phenolic content in natural products. Evolving from the Folin-Denis reagent—originally developed in the early 20th century to detect tyrosine in proteins—the F-C reagent is a complex mixture of phosphomolybdic and phosphotungstic heteropoly acids. It is prepared by reacting sodium tungstate and sodium molybdate with hydrochloric and phosphoric acids, stabilized by the addition of lithium sulfate. The reagent exhibits excellent stability when shielded from light and reducing agents.

The underlying chemistry of the F-C assay relies on an oxidation-reduction mechanism. In its active state, the molybdenum and tungsten metals exist in a 6+ oxidation state, giving the reagent its characteristic yellow color. Upon reacting with phenolic antioxidants, these metals are reduced to oxidation states between 5 and 6, producing a vibrant "molybdenum blue" or "tungsten blue" chromophore. While the reaction kinetics are sluggish under acidic conditions, the process accelerates significantly in basic environments. 

Although the method is highly sensitive and precise, it lacks absolute specificity for phenols. To refine this, Singleton and Rossi optimized the procedure using a molybdotungstophosphoric heteropolyanion that specifically targets polyphenols, with peak absorbance occurring at 765 nm. Reliability in the F-C assay depends heavily on standardized parameters, including specific volume ratios, incubation times, temperature control for color development, and the choice of a reference standard. Modern applications typically follow either a dual-reagent procedure or a simplified single-reagent protocol. In a standard manual dual-reagent setup, a diluted sample is mixed with the F-C reagent, followed by the addition of sodium carbonate to facilitate the reaction, with final absorbance measured after 0.5 to 2 hours of incubation.
humanized_abstract:
The Folin-Ciocalteau (F-C) assay is the most frequently employed assay for the determination of total phenolic content in plant materials due to its simplicity. The assay evolved from the Folin-Denis assay for tyrosine determination of proteins developed in the early twentieth century. The F-C reagent is a solution of a complex mixture of phosphomolybdic and phosphotungstic heteropoly acids formed by the reaction between sodium tungstate and sodium molybdate with hydrochloric acid and phosphoric acid, made alkaline with lithium sulfate solution, thus, it is light and reducing agents sensitive.

The F-C assay works on an oxidation-reduction reaction principle. In the active form, molybdenum and tungsten in the assay reagent are in the 6+ oxidation state responsible for the yellow colour. On reacting with phenolic antioxidants these metals are reduced to varying degrees (average 5-6) and are known as molybdenum blue or tungsten blue respectively. The slow speed under acidic conditions and the rapid speed under alkaline conditions is one of the distinctive features of this assay.

Although highly sensitive and precise, this method is not absolute in specificity for the target analytes, phenols. To improve this, Singleton and Rossi using a molybdotungstophosphoric heteropolyanion polyphenol-specific compound in the F-C assay. Assay reliability is heavily based on the standard operating conditions such as the optimized ratios of reagents, sample volume, time for the color to develop and temperature of color development in addition to the use of reference standard. Presently the F-C assay method is carried out using two sets of reagents — dual-reagent method and also by a simplified method of single-reagent. For manual determination of dual-reagent method, the procedure is as follows: A small amount of a dilute sample is poured in a test tube. F-C reagent is then added to it, to which sodium carbonate is subsequently added. The absorption reading of the solution formed after the reaction is then determined after a time range of 0.5-2 hours.
